messages.content:
[Rewrite  all texts in quotations to Baroque English; uses lots of thous, thees, thines, thys, etc.]

"I have just picked it up on the tip of my glove. If I put it down again, and it asks another ant, "What was that?" how would it explain? There are things in the universe billions of years older than either of our races. They are vast, timeless. And if they are aware of us at all, it is as little more than ants, and we have as much chance of communicating with them as an ant has with us! We know, we've tried! And we've learned we can either stay out from under foot, or be stepped on."

"I have but recently plucked it up upon the tip of mine glove. If I were to set it down once more, and it doth inquire of another ant, 'What hath transpired?' how shall it articulate? There exist entities in this vast universe, billions of years senior to either of our races. They are immense, eternal. And if perchance they dost acknowledge our existence, we are merely akin to insignificant ants, whilst we have no more probability of communicating with them than an ant doth possess with ourselves! Verily, we possess knowledge, we have made endeavours! And we have discovered that we can either cease meddling, or endure their wrath."
[More verbose, but condense it.]

"I have indeed acquired the subject upon the tip of mine glove. Should I relinquish it and inquire of a fellow ant regarding its nature, how shall I explain? In this vast universe, older than the existence of our respective races, dwell entities of immeasurable magnitude and timelessness. And if perchance they possess an awareness of our kind, it is but a mere semblance, viewing us as naught but insignificant ants. Alas, the possibility of establishing communication with them, akin to an ant endeavoring to converse with us, is nigh impossible! Our extensive endeavors have affirmed this truth. Henceforth, we must acquiesce to their dominion, lest we face their wrath as ants crushed beneath their weight."
[More verbose]

"Verily, I have indeed ascended to grasp this very entity upon the delicate tip of mine glove. Should I, perchance, release it unto the earthly plane once more and beseech a fellow ant to enlighten me with regards to its nature, how shall I adequately elucidate? Behold! In this boundless expanse known as the universe, its existence predates the birth of our own races by an inconceivable measure of billions of years. Resides therein celestial entities of colossal station, eternal in their essence. And if, in some miraculous fashion, they doth deign to acknowledge our feeble existence, it amounts to naught but a mere glimpse. Forsooth, in their eyes, we are akin to ants, pitiful and trivial insects, scarcely deserving of consideration. Alas, our paltry chances of forging any form of meaningful communion with them are as minuscule as an ant's futile attempt to converse with our own esteemed selves! We have been graced with this knowledge, for we have verily endeavored to establish contact! Yet we, mortals that we are, have gleaned naught but the calamitous truth. We must henceforth comprehend that we have naught to offer but surrender to their omniscient might, lest we suffer the dire consequences akin to ants futilely struggling beneath their relentless oppression."
[condense to four sentences]

I have grasped the entity upon my glove's delicate tip. If I release it and another ant inquires, how would I explain? Entities older than our races dwell in this vast universe, vast and timeless. If they are aware of us, we are insignificant ants to them, incapable of communication. We have tried, learned, and recognized our choice: to stay out from underfoot or be crushed.

NEW CONVERSATION

[rephrase text in quotations in flowery Elizabethan English; sprinkle lots of thous and thees along with archaic spellings of certain words.]

"I have just picked it up on the tip of my glove. If I put it down again, and it asks another ant, “What was that?” how would it explain? There are things in the universe billions of years older than either of our races. They are vast, timeless. And if they are aware of us at all, it is as little more than ants, and we have as much chance of communicating with them as an ant has with us! We know, we’ve tried! And we’ve learned we can either stay out from under foot, or be stepped on."

"I have most recently plucked it from yon tip of mine glove. Should I return it unto its former abode, only to be questioned by another ant, 'Pray, what hath cometh hence?' how shall it render explanation? In this vast expanse of the universe, entities of yore, spanning billions of years ere the advent of our kindred, doth reside. They, behemoth and timeless, doth eclipse the annals of our existence. And if, by some twist of fate, they doth hold cognizance of our being, we are mere ants, inconsequential and trifling, lacking the means to commune with their majestic presence! This we know, for we hath endeavored, and we hath discovered that we canst either tread discreetly or suffer the crushing blow of their disdain."
[More words need to be archaic]

"I have just now plucked it upon the tip of mine glove. Should I deposit it once more and query another humble ant, 'Prithee, what might that have been?' how shall it provide explanation? In this vast tapestry of the universe, there exist entities that supplant our very existence by spans of billions of years. They loom vast and eternal. And should they, perchance, deign to pay heed to our meager existence, we appear to them naught but minuscule creatures, mere ants in comparison, incapable of the most rudimentary forms of communion! We have, indeed, made attempts, and through these endeavors have we come to the realization that our options lie betwixt staying clear of their path or facing the inevitable trampling beneath their feet!"
[all words must be spelled as they were in Old English]

"Ic hæfde ðæt þuslice genumen uppan ðam gemete minre glówæ. Gi ic ðæt adown læte, and hit byðyre ānt oðer fram þearfe hæfe, "Hwæt wæs þæt?" hu scyle hit andswarian? Þær beoð ðing in ðære lyfte bylðenu gēola ældre ðonne begra gecynd. Hīe synt miccle, trymfulle. And gi hīe gewitan ðæt wit on cnāwun, hīe wit rǣda swā þeah swā āntas, and wit habba swā micel cēos þara to sprecenne swā ānt hæbbe mid us! Wit witon, wit hæft betr
[Okay, when I said Old English I meant Ye Olde Butcherede Englishe]

"I have but just now pickedet it up upon the tippe of mine glove. If I were to putte it down again, and it doth inquire of another ant, 'What was that?' how would it explaineth? There doth exist in the vast universe things that are billions of yeares older than either of our races. They are of a grand magnitude and timelesse nature. And if they are aware of us at all, it is as little more than ants, and we have as much chance of communicating with them as an ant hath with us! We dost knoweth, we hast tried! And we hast learned that we canst either stayeth out from under foot, or be steppeth on."
[More]

"I haveeth yond ance plucked it up anon the tippe of mine glove. Should I returneth it hence and prithee another yond ant, 'What was that?' how would it thusly expound? In the vastness of this yond universe, there existeth entities so hoar, spanning billions of yeares ere our races hath cometh to be. They are of yond immense and timeless nature. And if, by any hap, they dost taketh heed of our existence, we are to them but mere ants, and we possesseth as much fortune of communing with them as an ant hath with us! We doth knoweth, we hath essayed! And we hath learnedeth that we canst either taketh care to evade their path, or be trampled underfoot."
[More]

"Verily, I have just now plucked it up on the tip of mine glove. Should I return it hence and beseech another humble ant, 'What hath transpired?' how shall it expound? In the vast expanse of this cosmic realm, there doth exist entities of ancient provenance, spanning countless moons ere the dawn of our races. They loom in grandeur and standeth timeless. And if, perchance, they deign to take note of our very essence, we are to them like lowly ants, wherein the chances of communion betwixt us art as feeble as an ant attempting to converse with our own mortal coil! We knoweth, we hath ventured boldly! And we hath witnessed the reality that we shall either treadeth lightly or suffer the crushing blow underfoot!"
